With what currently exists, you only can manually update the tokens in the security object using the `EsiSecurity.update_token()` method.

As you manage the tokens yourself (and you don't want esipy to actually do something else than just updating the headers I believe), a simple solution I can see would be:
1. Create your security object
2. Create your client, giving him the security
3. Manually updating the client with (only) `access_token` and the `expires_in` value 

This way, you can use it normally, update it when necessary (and if you don't, it will throw an [AttributeError Exception](https://github.com/Kyria/EsiPy/blob/master/esipy/security.py#L161-L162) as the security object cannot update the token without refresh token)

For the callback, you can do it with the [signals](http://esipy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/advance/signals/). In your app just do something like this: 
```python
def callback_update_token(access_token, refresh_token, expires_in):
   # whatever you need it there

from esipy.event import after_token_refresh
after_token_refresh.add_receiver(callback_update_token)
```

Then, each time the token is automatically refreshed (when doing a `EsiClient.request()`), your `callback_update_token` function will be triggered. (I didn't set it for manual update, as i don't believe you need a callback when you manually update tokens).

Well, there will be a conflict if another library that's not compatible with Django decides to use glom.default_app_config, load the module it contains as a string, and expect anything else than a Django App, otherwise, everything will be fine.
Otherwise, we could have a django app in glom/django_glom, then the user can add glom.django_glom to INSTALLED_APPS and benefit from auto registration with the App class, that could also autodiscover glom.py files from INSTALLED_APPS like Django already does for the admin or models.
Otherwise, you can move glom/ to src/glom and change the setup script to it install all modules from src/, then it will install src/glom_django, src/glom_sqlalchemy, etc ... you can see examples in yourlabs/django-autocomplete-light or yourlabs/crudlfap, their setup.py install all modules in src/ subdirectory which is great to keep control on depencency coupling.

Original comment by: Alan Franzoni

I would prefer not to require any interactive input. Although there's value in using a single password to manage many, a primary purpose of keyring is to provide programmatic access to a secure credential set, such that an application (GUI or command-line or cron script) can access the OS-protected keyring.

I would like for the user's already authenticated session to be the guard against unauthorized use of the keyring. I wouldn't mind this behavior being the default for a subprocess-based keyring if there were already implemented a direct API (such as through ctypes), but the direct API keyring would still be the default for OS X.
Will it work with the already saved items from older python-keyring versions?

Debian doesn't have the problem since they decided to not include python-secretstorage in the build environment, because they
"have not found a good a way to run tests that require dbus in a chroot".

I would suggest making that test optional or mocking dbus or other parts.

The only other option I have as a package maintainer is removing the tests from packaging. So problems with conflicting apis/versions/whatever can only be found on usage later on.

OK...  

So this is what I have done:

+ You can create more than 1 `App` objectin a guizero program and it acts 'normally'
+ However you receive a warning when creating multiple App objects (there should only really be 1)
+ There is a new `Window` class which when needs to be passed the an `App` when created
+ `Windows` belong to `App`'s
+ `Windows` can be made `visible` using `show` and `hide`
+ `Window.show()` accepts an optional `wait` parameter which will force the calling application to have to `wait` until this window is closed (creating a modal window)
+ in other ways `Window` acts very much like an `App`

To create a multi window application you would:

```python
from guizero import App, Window, PushButton

# app
app = App(title="Main window")

# 2nd window
window = Window(app, title="2nd window")
window.hide()

# modal window
modal_window = Window(app, title="modal window")
modal_window.hide()

# buttons to open windows
open_window_button = PushButton(app, text="Open window", command=window.show)
open_modal_button = PushButton(app, text="Open modal window", command=modal_window.show, args = [True])

# buttons to close windows
close_window_button = PushButton(window, text="Close", command=window.hide)
close_modal_button = PushButton(modal_window, text="Close", command=modal_window.hide)

app.display()

```

The pep details some constraints:

- frozendict has to implement the Mapping abstract base class
- frozendict keys and values can be unorderable
- a frozendict is hashable if all keys and values are hashable
- frozendict hash does not depend on the items creation order

Most implementations referenced in the Pep don't match the constraints fully; this may need touched up but I think it does (although it's not implemented in C which would be even cooler).

An aside on the ipython console:

I don't know if people use watermark from the ipython console, but if so, simply installing ipython in the virtualenv may not work if the system ipython has been run previously within the virtualenv. Here is an example:

1. Create and activate a fresh virtualenv, do *not* install ipython in the virtualenv
2. Launch the ipython console
    - the system ipython will be called (but like jupyter it recognizes that you're in a virtualenv so typical imports of virtualenv installed packages are fine)
3. Exit ipython
4. Now `pip install watermark`, which installs ipython
5. `which ipython` will report the virtualenv ipython executable. So we're good, right?
6. Launch an ipython console
7. `import sys; sys.argv` shows that the system ipython console is still being used 
8. `watermark`/`pkg_resources` refer to system level packages, not those in the virtualenv

Yo, 
You're of course right when you're mentioning that there's a lot of work in single channel separation. Actually, I was making an oversimplification here. 
The point is actually on whether we are evaluating over the "sources" or the "images" as references. This can happen in both single channel and multichannel separation. Let I denote the number of chanels of the mixture (I=1 for mono, I=2 for stereo). I'll just remind the difference: 
* object images are usually understood as the sound of an object *as it appears in the mix*. By definition, the mix is the sum of the object images. Hence, these images are each one a sound with I channels. *Note that in the case of mono, you can still define images, that are then also single channel waveforms*.
*  object sources are usually understood as the underlying *single channel* signal that was subsequently filtered to yield the object image. 
In bss_eval_images, you are directly comparing the reference (I channels each) to the estimated objects (I channels each)
In bss_eval_sources, you want to compare the mono  references with the mono estimated sources. 
In the single channel case, you could think these yield the same estimates, but it is not so: there is this idea in bss_eval_sources that the sound you provide as ground truth has been filtered before yielding the image against which you should be comparing. Each time, you are assuming that this filter is identical to the best linear filter that goes from the provided reference to the estimate.
This means that the reference sound against which you are comparing is modified according to the test sound each time bss_eval_sources is called.

In practice, you can get infinite SDR by bss_eval_sources by just zeroing frequencies: the reference sound will then be estimated as 0 and you should get a pretty good score...

What I wanted to say was hence not really a matter of dropping mono evaluations, but rather of dropping this estimation of the best filter that is is bss_eval_sources. Anyways, most corpora I am aware of are actually provided the images of the objects: the mix is the simple sum of them

Yep, that is the key question :). In our case, we have a significant amount of logic that needs to be run to initialize a webapp. Several external (to the webapp, at least) resources that need to be loaded and many python modules that need to be imported. We load those pre-fork in order to get them into shared memory and greatly decrease the webapp's memory footprint. The method of loading these external resources is not entirely up to our control (or is up to our control but is not easily modified), and depends on crochet/twisted.

Regarding your earlier point on `get_reactor` being an additional burden on the user: this change maintains full backwards compatibility. If `crochet.setup()` is called, then the global Twisted reactor is still used and the user is free to do `from twisted.internet import reactor` to get a reference to the crochet reactor. Only if `crochet.setup(use_global_reactor=True`) is used is the user required to use `get_reactor` to get a reference to crochet's reactor.

By its nature, a git filter can only act on the commit about to be made. It's not designed to "spawn" another commit in a different branch (I suppose it could be made to do that with some trickery, but I rather wouldn't like to implement such a "hack" in nbstripout).

You can however emulate the behaviour you want by installing nbstripout only in a `.gitattributes` file in your `release` branch. Assuming your "dirty" notebooks are on master, you can create a clean branch as follows:

Create an orphan release branch and install nbstripout in that branch only:
```
git checkout --orphan release
nbstripout --install --attributes=.gitattributes
git add .gitattributes
git commit -m 'Install nbstripout'
```
You can then cherry-pick notebook commits as follows (you will probably have to do this in order to avoid merge conflicts, unless each commit is entirely self contained):
```
git cherry-pick --no-commit
git commit -a --no-edit
```
From my quick test those 2 stages are necessary for the filter to kick in i.e. if you just do a plain cherry pick the filter is not applied.

Hope this helps!

Writing this here for future references.

For starters, you have to realise that `bot.run` is an abstraction of a couple of more lower level concepts.

There is `Client.login` which logs in the client and then `Client.connect` which actually runs the processing. These are coroutines.

`asyncio` provides the capability of putting things in the event loop for it to work whenever it has time to.

Something like this e.g.

```py
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

async def foo():
  await asyncio.sleep(10)
  loop.close()

loop.create_task(foo())
loop.run_forever()
```

If we want to wait for something to happen from another coroutine, asyncio provides us with this functionality as well ([docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-sync.html)) through the means of synchronisation via `asyncio.Event`. You can consider this as a boolean that you are waiting for:

```py
e = asyncio.Event()
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

async def foo():
  await e.wait()
  print('we are done waiting...')
  loop.stop()

async def bar():
  await asyncio.sleep(20)
  e.set()

loop.create_task(bar())
loop.create_task(foo())
loop.run_forever() # foo will stop this event loop when 'e' is set to true
loop.close()
```

It also provides us with the means to wait for multiple 'futures' or coroutines to complete ([docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html#asyncio.wait)):

```py
a = asyncio.Event()
b = asyncio.Event()

async def foo():
  await asyncio.sleep(10)
  a.set()

async def bar():
  await asyncio.sleep(15)
  b.set()

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.create_task(bar())
loop.create_task(foo())
loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.wait([a.wait(), b.wait()]))
loop.close()
```

Using this concept we can apply it to the discord bots themselves.



```py
import asyncio
import discord
from collections import namedtuple

# First, we must attach an event signalling when the bot has been
# closed to the client itself so we know when to fully close the event loop.

Entry = namedtuple('Entry', 'client event')
entries = [
  Entry(client=discord.Client(), event=asyncio.Event()),
  Entry(client=discord.Client(), event=asyncio.Event())
]

# Then, we should login to all our clients and wrap the connect call
# so it knows when to do the actual full closure

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

async def login():
  for e in entries:
    await e.client.login()

async def wrapped_connect(entry):
  try:
    await entry.client.connect()
  except Exception as e:
    await entry.client.close()
    print('We got an exception: ', e.__class__.__name__, e)
    entry.event.set()

# actually check if we should close the event loop:
async def check_close():
  futures = [e.event.wait() for e in entries]
  await asyncio.wait(futures)

# here is when we actually login
loop.run_until_complete(login())

# now we connect to every client
for entry in entries:
  loop.create_task(wrapped_connect(entry))

# now we're waiting for all the clients to close
loop.run_until_complete(check_close())

# finally, we close the event loop
loop.close()
```

Hope this helps.

Date values are parsed into an [epoch (or Unix) time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix_time) for easier/faster sorting.

To convert the dates, use `new Date()`, you can test these in the browser console:

```js
new Date(1523329200000)
//=> Mon Apr 09 2018 22:00:00 GMT-0500 (Central Daylight Time)

new Date(1523674799999)
//=> Fri Apr 13 2018 21:59:59 GMT-0500 (Central Daylight Time)
```

You should be able to handle the conversion on either the client or server side.

Hello, I looked into this and I think the root of the issue is that `atexit` is not always called.

The [atexit docs](https://docs.python.org/2/library/atexit.html) state normal program termination should call the functions..

> At normal program termination (for instance, if sys.exit() is called or the main module’s execution completes), all functions registered are called in last in, first out order.

..but receiving a kill signal doesn't qualify

These are the default handlers for different kill signals:

```
>>> import signal
>>> for i in range(1,16):
...     print(i, signal.getsignal(i))
...
(1, 0)
(2, <built-in function default_int_handler>)
(3, 0)
(4, 0)
(5, 0)
(6, 0)
(7, 0)
(8, 0)
(9, None)
(10, 0)
(11, 0)
(12, 0)
(13, 1)
(14, 0)
(15, 0)
```

So [ctrl-c], (keyboard interrupt) will trigger a `sys.exit()`, but nothing else will until you set the handler yourself.

Signals with `0` use the default handler, which does nothing and ends python.
Signals with `1` ignoring the signal without even exiting
9 always exits without being handled

If a python process receives a kill signal for a signal with the default do-nothing handler, none of the `atexit` registered functions will ever be called, including the `logging`'s `shutdown`, which @kislyuk mentioned.

If you register a handler which does nothing but call `sys.exit()`, then all `atexit` registered functions are called, and the logs should flush.

The problem with importing other packages is that FanFicFare has three versions -- the (pip installed) CLI, the Google Cloud web service and the Calibre plugin.  Only the CLI can require additional packages; the CLI and plugin must carry their own copies of packages they want if not already present in the environment.

This, plus the fact Python's built-in datetime libraries expect parsed dates to match the local machine's locale, means there's already a lot of code included to work around datetime issues.  Take a look at what [makeDate](https://github.com/JimmXinu/FanFicFare/blob/master/fanficfare/adapters/base_adapter.py#L705) does with month abbrev names and AM/PM for example.

So I'm not inclined to add new libraries if it can be avoided.

Short answer: Manually write a parse function--unless it's crazy difficult, then we could talk about it more.

Let me explain my real trading (long-term strategy, no intraday):
Monday evening:
The broker give me the last price of a share (close price). With this price and all the other close prices the days before I calculate a signal. For example it is telling me to buy. Market is closed, I have to wait.

Tuesday morning:
The broker give me the first price (open price). With this price I can calculate the number of shares and I can open a order without getting a margin error. (**backtrader uses the close price of monday not the actual price and maybe my order failed to open**).

```python
from numpy import dtype

rec = Recorder('measurement.mf4')

    ChannelDescriptor('CH1', 's', 'first channel', dtype('S')),
    ChannelDescriptor('CH2', 'm', 'second channel', dtype('<u4')),
    ChannelDescriptor('CH3', 'rpm', 'third channel', dtype('<f2')),
]

deposit1 = rec.register(channels)


# second source
channels = [
    ChannelDescriptor('CH_A', 'mph', 'first channel', dtype('>u8')),
    ChannelDescriptor('CH_B', 'rad', 'second channel', dtype('<i4')),
    ChannelDescriptor('CH_C', 'us', 'third channel', dtype('<f8')),
]

deposit2 = rec.register(channels)

# start measurement

while 1:
    
    if source1.has_samples:
        # source samples is a list of python int, float or str objects
        deposit1.append(source1.samples)
        
    if source2.has_samples:
        # source samples is a list of python int, float or str objects
        deposit2.append(source2.samples) 
        
    # stop measurement when some condition is True
    if stop_condition:
        break
    
# stop recorder and the measurement is saved automatically

rec.stop()
```

You have to make sure to keep the sample values in the same order as the one used for registering to the Recorder object.

Everything is kept in the RAM until the _stop_ method is called. At this point the MF4 file is created and the used RAM should be freed.

Hi @adamkpickering,

The problem with that solution is that when you instantiate a iperf3.Server object, the port number is just stored in the port variable. It doesn't reserve any port on the network stack of the machine. The actual server port will only be opened when starting the server (server.run()). 

Building in a check during instantiation will not provide any guarantee that the port isn't taken when you call the Server.run() function. Let's me try to illustrate:

Say you have built in a check for this when you set the server port variable :

```
server = iperf.Server()

# Here we set the port and lets say the module checks and sees no process is running on port 1234
server.port = 1234

# Now we do some more stuff in the script before actually starting the server
# but during this process some other process on the machine starts a server
# on port 1234
sleep(10)

# You finished your 10 seconds of sleep, think all is well and run the server...
#  Unfortunately the port is now already taken and you still get
# an error.
server.run()
```

I don't have a machine available at the moment to test but to solve your issue
I would suggest to do something like this:

```
server = iperf.Server()
server.port = 1234

try:
   server.run()
except NotSureWhatExceptionGetsRaisedByDefaultForPortInuse:
  server.port += 1
  server.run()
```

Cheers,

You have created a fantastic package which is simple and does it job.
But to be honest, my first impression was an 'has no attribute'-error. 
The authorization failed and some "random" instructions crashed the whole process.
We have to blame microsoft for the design of this auth process.
But sharepy lacks proper error handling, too.

Nevertheless, I am very pleased with your library (I could connect to sharepoint in less than 5 minutes) and your effort to fix and improve it.
You designed sharepy with certain goals in mind. I think some aspects can be tweaked to make sharepy even better:
* The repo does not include the package code (e.g. setup.py and requirements.txt). I think, requests is missing as a dependency.
* Print error messages to console if in interactive mode. Raise exceptions otherwise. I use sharepy in a small python script at work which runs automatically. I never see the output unless the script crashses. Same for the password prompt.

Regarding the auth domain: I expect users of this library to know what "kind" of sharepoint they are connecting to. Good question. A few options, each with pros and cons:
- Development time (static): run a script that autogenerates some python or a `.json` or `.yaml` file that contains the filter info. _Pros_: eliminates runtime overhead. _Cons_: different ffmpeg builds may have different filters, so some of the filters might not be available for whoever's installing `ffmpeg-python`.
- Installation time (semi-dynamic): generate filter info when `ffmpeg-python` pip package is being installed. _Pros_: runs ffmpeg on the system of whoever's installing it and thus has accurate list of filters. _Cons_: may not use the ffmpeg executable (which is probably a showstopper, since the correct executable may not even be available at installation time).
- Runtime (dynamic): run ffmpeg as soon as `ffmpeg-python` is imported. _Pros_: has accurate list of filters. _Cons_: adds runtime overhead and either uses the wrong ffmpeg executable or requires additional code changes.

So I'm thinking static is the only approach that really makes sense.

Ah, I think I know what's going on here.

We first call `FindItem` to list all items in a folder. This is fine since private items are allowed to be listed there, with the private fields properly anonymized.

Then we call `GetItem` to fetch all fields for the items returned by `FindItem`. This fails for private items because we, among other fields, request the `body` field, which is private.

You can test if this is indeed the case by changing your query to only fetch non-private fields:

```python
for item in items.all().only('start', 'end').order_by('start'):
    print(item.start, item.end)
```

```python
    'bagging_fraction': 0.8,
    'bagging_freq': 100
}

}
```

```python
search_log_file = output_folder + 'light-GBM-optimization-5.csv'
search_log_cols = list(params.keys()) + ['target']
search_log = pd.DataFrame(columns=search_log_cols)

def xgb_evaluate(**kwargs):
    global search_log
    
    # set params we've found
    new_x = {}
    new_x.update(fixed_params)
    
    # set params we are checking right now
    new_x.update(kwargs)
    
    # fix decimal places and ranges
    new_x['max_bin'] = int(new_x['max_bin'])
    new_x['num_leaves'] = int(new_x['num_leaves'])
    new_x['min_data_in_leaf'] = int(new_x['min_data_in_leaf'])
    new_x['feature_fraction'] = max(min(new_x['feature_fraction'], 1), 0)
    new_x['bagging_fraction'] = max(min(new_x['bagging_fraction'], 1), 0)
    new_x['bagging_freq'] = int(new_x['bagging_freq'])
    
    print('starting cv with parameters: ' + str(new_x))

    search_log.drop_duplicates(search_log_cols[:-1], inplace=True)
    
    # add row with new parameters to log
    i = search_log.shape[0]
    search_log.loc[i] = pd.DataFrame([new_x], columns=search_log_cols).loc[0]
    
    # if we already tested these parameters then drop last row and return existing result
    # ...
    
    # train and validate    
    scores = []
    for i, (train_index, test_index) in enumerate(cv_folds):        
        gbmr = getRegressor()
        gbmr.param.update(new_x)
        
        X_train, X_val = X_train_scaled.iloc[train_index], X_train_scaled.iloc[test_index]
        y_train, y_val = Y_train.iloc[train_index], Y_train.iloc[test_index]

        gbmr.fit(X_train, y_train)
        y_pred = gbmr.predict(X_val)
        cv = evalerrorGridSearch(y_val, y_pred)
        
        scores.append(cv)

    result = np.array(scores).mean()
    print('CV score: ' + str(result))
    
    # save new score to last row of the log
    last_item_i = search_log.shape[0] - 1
    search_log.loc[last_item_i][-1] = result
    
    # save to file
    search_log.to_csv(search_log_file, index=None)
    
    return -result
```

Google Cloud backend's `def url(self, name):` makes a round trip to Google's APIs and complete breaks all views and APIs that list several images. This is *not* what most Django devs would expect especially when considering how the default File storage in Django works (url is a matter of computing settings.MEDIA_URL and the file's name stored in database). It's in fact quite common in Django to do something akin to: ``` # models.py @property def cover_photo_url(self): if self.cover_photo: return self.cover_photo.url return '' # or some default # template ``` Now I do understand that `.name` exists and we can just use that to compute the URL ourselves. However, the documentation doesn't mention this at all which is what really bothers me. I had to waste precious time profiling my API to see what's causing bad performance.

determine "lazy" types. In the given case, if pythran_fooa0 is a lazy type,
as it alias on c, c is a lazy type and they will be evaluated 4 times (it
is bad for lazy type like numpy_expr). So we assume that it is not lazy.
But it is based on lazy type which is stricter than FS. We should certainly
looks only for variable name instead of aliasing in this case.

To recap:, the survival package in R implements a conditional logit with
clogit. That clogit just calls a coxph, using the 'exact' method. From
coxph's documentation:
""The “exact partial likelihood” is equivalent to a conditional logistic
model, and is appropriate when the times are a small set of discrete
values. If there are a large number of ties and (start, stop) style
survival data the computational time will be excessive."


I started talking with the statsmodels folks, and we determined that
'exact' vs 'efron' is a significant difference. Aside from 'exact' and
'efron' producing different results, the 'exact' method is much faster when
the data is the right shape for a conditional logit. See this thread on the
pystatsmodels Google Group for the conversation:
https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pystatsmodels/o9ZwTPInnuc

Josef at statsmodels implemented a ConditionalLogit over the past two days;
you can see our conversation in the Google Group. Here is the current state
of things, from Josef:

"
I put the module with the class into `miscmodels` which contains
GenericLikelihoodModel subclasses (those are models that are missing
specific bells and whistles)

https://github.com/josef-pkt/statsmodels/commit/5242d49a1cbc2ee8656ea6714d91b7198bf03ba7
<https://www.google.com/url?q=https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2Fjosef-pkt%2Fstatsmodels%2Fcommit%2F5242d49a1cbc2ee8656ea6714d91b7198bf03ba7&sa=D&sntz=1&usg=AFQjCNEoau6iabT8vvxAfxB8vratGcLqrg>

an the script with examples is in a gist for now
https://gist.github.com/josef-pkt/428c3c671c400f5b9cb2
<https://www.google.com/url?q=https%3A%2F%2Fgist.github.com%2Fjosef-pkt%2F428c3c671c400f5b9cb2&sa=D&sntz=1&usg=AFQjCNFALriV2mzzgJcJaU2ZPwEsHSRvTg>
"

Possible next steps would be to collaborate with statsmodels and the
freshly written code there, or to implement ones own 'exact' method. The

Nice discussion and good followups.

Regarding c-index, which I'm specializing to "concordance" in CoxPH: if I
understood correctly once the vector of trained weights, beta in R^n, is
fixed then the "risk" of a member with value x in R^n is the value
exp(x.beta) in R. This risk is not a function of time. When computing
concordance on pairs, we compare pairs of members' respective observed
event times with this risk.

On the other hand, in the Aalen model beta is not a point in R^n but rather
beta(t): T -> R^n where the domain T is a subset of of the non-negative
reals R^\geq 0. Therefore, exp(x.beta(t)) depends on time and we don't
(immediately) get a single"risk" score as in CoxPH.

Does that makes sense? In nutshell: what's the appropriate generalization
of "risk" if beta(t) depends on time?

I don't know about adoption in general, but in our case we're connecting to an existing service which uses the 0.9 protocol, a [recent changes](https://wikitech.wikimedia.org/wiki/RCStream) feed for Wikimedia projects.  I see some [discussion](https://phabricator.wikimedia.org/T68232) which concludes that the server will be upgraded to use the new protocol when a Debian package exists for python-(gevent-)socketio that supports 1.0.

Unfortunately, that project is [not planning](https://github.com/abourget/gevent-socketio/issues/192) socketio 1.0 integration, however there is an [independent rewrite](https://github.com/shuoli84/gevent_socketio2) in progress which might fill the gap.  This indicates that Python projects are probably locked into 0.9 for the near future, although I expect that nodejs projects have fully embraces 1.0.

In the meantime, we're happy to use the old version of your library, but I see how it would be logistically annoying to support packages built from multiple versions.  They would have to have different names, to start with...  I'm sure this patch will apply to master as well, I was hoping to port it with your agreement.

How difficult do you expect it would be to support both protocols in the newer code?  Less annoying than dealing with split version packaging?

```python
def vhasdoc(self, other_version):
    """Return True if the other version has the current document. Like Sphinx's hasdoc().

    :raise KeyError: If other_version doesn't exist.

    :param str other_version: Version to link to.

    :return: If current document is in the other version.
    :rtype: bool
    """
    if self.context['current_version'] == other_version:
        return True
    other_remote = self[other_version]
    return self.context['pagename'] in other_remote['found_docs']

def vpathto(self, other_version):
    """Return relative path to current document in another version. Like Sphinx's pathto().

    If the current document doesn't exist in the other version its master_doc path is returned instead.

    :raise KeyError: If other_version doesn't exist.

    :param str other_version: Version to link to.

    :return: Relative path.
    :rtype: str
    """
    current_version = self.context['current_version']
    has_doc = self.vhasdoc(other_version)
    is_root_ref = self.context['scv_is_root_ref']
    pagename = self.context['pagename']
    if current_version == other_version and not is_root_ref and has_doc:
        return '{}.html'.format(pagename.split('/')[-1])  # User already in this directory, just link to other file.
    level = pagename.count('/')
    if not has_doc:
        pagename = self[other_version]['master_doc']
    if is_root_ref:
        return '{}.html'.format('/'.join(['..'] * level + [other_version, pagename]))
    return '{}.html'.format('/'.join(['..'] * level + ['..', other_version, pagename]))
```

As a first proof of concept and workaround I am using the Levenshtein distance algorithm to compare the deepspeech result with a grammar string. The higher the number, the better the match. It will only work with lower cases, no punktuation and you need to add some code around to get a percentage value depending on the stringlength.

https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Algorithm_Implementation/Strings/Levenshtein_distance

```bash
$ cat levenshtein.sh 
#!/bin/bash

#https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Algorithm_Implementation/Strings/Levenshtein_distance
function levenshtein {
    if [ "$#" -ne "2" ]; then
        echo "Usage: $0 word1 word2" >&2
    elif [ "${#1}" -lt "${#2}" ]; then
        levenshtein "$2" "$1"
    else
        local str1len=$((${#1}))
        local str2len=$((${#2}))
        local d i j
        for i in $(seq 0 $(((str1len+1)*(str2len+1)))); do
            d[i]=0
        done
        for i in $(seq 0 $((str1len))); do
            d[$((i+0*str1len))]=$i
        done
        for j in $(seq 0 $((str2len))); do
            d[$((0+j*(str1len+1)))]=$j
        done

        for j in $(seq 1 $((str2len))); do
            for i in $(seq 1 $((str1len))); do
                [ "${1:i-1:1}" = "${2:j-1:1}" ] && local cost=0 || local cost=1
                local del=$((d[(i-1)+str1len*j]+1))
                local ins=$((d[i+str1len*(j-1)]+1))
                local alt=$((d[(i-1)+str1len*(j-1)]+cost))
                d[i+str1len*j]=$(echo -e "$del\n$ins\n$alt" | sort -n | head -1)
            done
        done
        echo ${d[str1len+str1len*(str2len)]}
    fi
}

levenshtein "$1" "$2"
```

The problem is you need to store the state somewhere. And the design philosophy of Luigi as Elias mentioned is that the "worker knows best". That's why typically you implement this check by relying on the file system. In fact you can implement it in any way you want by using the `completed()` function. But the default implementation of `completed()` is to run the `output()` function and check if the output exists.

So you have full control over this behavior, you just need to be explicit about where the state is stored.

The benefit of this approach is that it's also explicit what to do if you want to re-run something – just delete the file (or whatever marker you use, could be a row in a database or anything) and then re-run the dependency graph

Also broken on Linux x64. The cause is in CMakeLists.txt, in the line 170s area. `CMAKE_C_FLAGS` _must_ include `-fPIC` when linking shared libs on 64 bit, due to the overall large size of the project. Also, the reason that this was originally gated on `CMAKE_SIZEOF_VOID` is that it was thought that certain deploy environments, such as ARM 32 bit, are often space constrained enough that the smaller binary possible when skipping `fPIC` would be worth effort.

I guess that was a poorly phrased question on too little sleep...sorry.  I feel like this functionality exists, I'm just not using the `origin` or initializing correctly or something.

The following is what I did, happy to adapt it to the test file that the docs are coming from if you think it is correct.  The framework essentially calls three methods in order:

1. initialize everything (`git init`, add README and gitignore)
2. copy a bunch of files and `git add` them with the `Repo` object
3. commit and push

Cutting out a large amount of logging and error checking, it looks something like this

```
import os
import shutil
from git import Repo

# leave_early() should be viewed as equivalent to `assert`
# or throwing an exception. it's returning a tuple of
# strings and other logging related items
def leave_early(msg):
    raise Exception(msg)

#
# input to all phase methods
###############################
repo_path      = "path/to/top/level"
git_directory  = os.path.abspath(repo_path)
secret_url     = "https://<oauth>@<github_url>/<github_org>/<repo_name>.git"
commit_message = "Some message about what is being dispatched."

#
# init phase
###############################
repo = Repo.init(git_directory)
if repo.__class__ is not Repo:
    leave_early("Unable to create repository [{}]".format(git_directory))

# Equivalent of `git remote add origin <secret url>`
origin = repo.create_remote('origin', secret_url)
if not origin.exists():
    leave_early("Unable to add the remote `origin {}`.".format(secret_url))

# my framework does text processing on the input readme,
# docs example maybe better to just assume valid paths
readme_source = "/path/to/input/README.md"
readme_dest   = "{}/README.md".format(git_directory)
try:
    shutil.copy(readme_source, readme_dest)
except Exception as e:
    leave_early("Unable to copy from [{}] to [{}]".format(readme_source, readme_dest))
# Add the README.md to the index of the git repository
readme_path = os.path.join(repo.working_tree_dir, "README.md")
repo.index.add([readme_path])

# (rinse lather and repeat for .gitignore)
# [[[ ... not included ... ]]]

# (then an arbitrary amount of files are copied into the directory)
# repo.index.add([list of paths to these])

#
# finalize phase
###############################
origin = repo.remotes.origin
repo.index.commit(commit_message)
git = repo.git
git.execute(["git", "push", "-u", "origin", "master"])
```

The only thing I can think of is that something goes wrong when `init` happens `origin = repo.create_remote('origin', secret_url)`, whereas in finalize I just grab `repo.remotes.origin`, but that seems unlikely.

Let me know what your thoughts are.

I thought about your argument, and I still think the PyPI metadata for 2.6 should be added, as long as it seems to work on 2.6. In case some unsurmountable problem occurs on 2.6, (or even if a problem occurs that could be solved but that you don't *want* to solve anymore), then you can make the decision at that point to drop support for 2.6 and you can remove the metadata tag for 2.6 again in a release update.

If tools parse the metadata tag as long as it is on a release, they conclude the package can be used on 2.6, which is what you want to express with it.

I think the real concern is the unknowns of potential future problems, and whether they can be solved if they occur. My take there is simply as long as you don't know of any problems, it works. And you can only tell whether a problem can be solved or not when it has occurred.

I'm not aware of any host libraries which implement the 1.1 specification, but the changes are quite minimal:

> CHANGES: This version version 1.1 of the FIDO U2F JavaScript API specification supersedes version JavaScript API 1.0. The major difference between these two versions is the way that requests to be signed are formatted between the RP and the client: In version 1.0, a separate appId and challenge were sent for every keyHandle, whereas in version 1.1, an optimization is made that requires only a single appId and challenge for multiple keyHandles.

The specs are available here: https://fidoalliance.org/specs/fido-u2f-v1.1-id-20160915/fido-u2f-javascript-api-v1.1-id-20160915.html

As for communicating with a server, it really depends on the application. For example, in a browser setting the messages are commonly passed as part of a HTML page, including the code to interact with the JavaScript API.

OpenKey throws a WindowsError if the registry key it's looking for can't be found in python2. So in python2, where WindowsError is distinct from and not a subclass of IOError, a lookup attempt on a missing registry key is not caught and crashes setuptools. Yeah that seems reasonable, though waiting even without the expectation of `.+` is probably sufficient too?

The price we pay for this approach, though, is that checks which test for rejection take longer, right? e.g. Mario, which tests for rejection four times, would have a check that takes four seconds longer (as opposed to it taking ~2 seconds right now locally). And I’m not sure I’d feel comfortable using less than a 1 second timeout because we’ve seen cases where due to ECS load, we sometimes hit short timeouts even when under normal conditions we wouldn’t.

If we’re willing to accept the additional time to check (or willing to take the risk of cutting down on the timeout to something shorter), then I think this makes sense? Once we parallelize checks, that might make the time issue here less important.

The SBP messages classes are hard to use in Haskell right now. We need to come up with a better way to ingest and digest binary based messages, and have a good think about how we want to represent messages in our system - do we carry on with the same MsgEnvelope containing SubMsg behavior as in python - or do we want to try something different out. I've made a couple of passed at this, and their both kind of  icky. Here's the latest:

```haskell
import BasicPrelude hiding ( length)
import Data.Binary
import Data.Binary.Get
import Data.Binary.Put
import Data.ByteString hiding ( putStrLn )
import Data.ByteString.Lazy hiding ( ByteString, putStrLn, length )
import SwiftNav.SBP.Acquisition
import SwiftNav.SBP.Bootload
import SwiftNav.SBP.ExtEvents
import SwiftNav.SBP.FileIo
import SwiftNav.SBP.Flash
import SwiftNav.SBP.Logging
import SwiftNav.SBP.Navigation
import SwiftNav.SBP.Observation
import SwiftNav.SBP.Piksi
import SwiftNav.SBP.Settings
import SwiftNav.SBP.System
import SwiftNav.SBP.Tracking

msgSBPPreamble :: Word8
msgSBPPreamble = 0x55

data MsgUnknown = MsgUnknown
  { msgUnknownType    :: Word16
  , msgUnknownPayload :: ByteString
  } deriving ( Show, Read, Eq )

data SBPMsg =
     SBPMsgAcqResult MsgAcqResult
   | SBPMsgAcqResultDepA MsgAcqResultDepA
   | SBPMsgAlmanac MsgAlmanac
   | SBPMsgBasePos MsgBasePos
   | SBPMsgBaselineEcef MsgBaselineEcef
   | SBPMsgBaselineNed MsgBaselineNed
   | SBPMsgBootloaderHandshakeDepA MsgBootloaderHandshakeDepA
   | SBPMsgBootloaderHandshakeReq MsgBootloaderHandshakeReq
   | SBPMsgBootloaderHandshakeResp MsgBootloaderHandshakeResp
   | SBPMsgBootloaderJumpToApp MsgBootloaderJumpToApp
   | SBPMsgCwResults MsgCwResults
   | SBPMsgCwStart MsgCwStart
   | SBPMsgDops MsgDops
   | SBPMsgEphemeris MsgEphemeris
   | SBPMsgEphemerisDepA MsgEphemerisDepA
   | SBPMsgEphemerisDepB MsgEphemerisDepB
   | SBPMsgExtEvent MsgExtEvent
   | SBPMsgFileioReadDirReq MsgFileioReadDirReq
   | SBPMsgFileioReadDirResp MsgFileioReadDirResp
   | SBPMsgFileioReadReq MsgFileioReadReq
   | SBPMsgFileioReadResp MsgFileioReadResp
   | SBPMsgFileioRemove MsgFileioRemove
   | SBPMsgFileioWriteReq MsgFileioWriteReq
   | SBPMsgFileioWriteResp MsgFileioWriteResp
   | SBPMsgFlashDone MsgFlashDone
   | SBPMsgFlashErase MsgFlashErase
   | SBPMsgFlashProgram MsgFlashProgram
   | SBPMsgFlashReadReq MsgFlashReadReq
   | SBPMsgFlashReadResp MsgFlashReadResp
   | SBPMsgGpsTime MsgGpsTime
   | SBPMsgHeartbeat MsgHeartbeat
   | SBPMsgIarState MsgIarState
   | SBPMsgInitBase MsgInitBase
   | SBPMsgM25FlashWriteStatus MsgM25FlashWriteStatus
   | SBPMsgMaskSatellite MsgMaskSatellite
   | SBPMsgNapDeviceDnaReq MsgNapDeviceDnaReq
   | SBPMsgNapDeviceDnaResp MsgNapDeviceDnaResp
   | SBPMsgObs MsgObs
   | SBPMsgObsDepA MsgObsDepA
   | SBPMsgPosEcef MsgPosEcef
   | SBPMsgPosLlh MsgPosLlh
   | SBPMsgPrint MsgPrint
   | SBPMsgReset MsgReset
   | SBPMsgResetFilters MsgResetFilters
   | SBPMsgSetTime MsgSetTime
   | SBPMsgSettingsReadByIndexDone MsgSettingsReadByIndexDone
   | SBPMsgSettingsReadByIndexReq MsgSettingsReadByIndexReq
   | SBPMsgSettingsReadByIndexResp MsgSettingsReadByIndexResp
   | SBPMsgSettingsReadReq MsgSettingsReadReq
   | SBPMsgSettingsReadResp MsgSettingsReadResp
   | SBPMsgSettingsSave MsgSettingsSave
   | SBPMsgSettingsWrite MsgSettingsWrite
   | SBPMsgStartup MsgStartup
   | SBPMsgStmFlashLockSector MsgStmFlashLockSector
   | SBPMsgStmFlashUnlockSector MsgStmFlashUnlockSector
   | SBPMsgStmUniqueIdReq MsgStmUniqueIdReq

   | SBPMsgThreadState MsgThreadState

   | SBPMsgVelEcef MsgVelEcef
   | SBPMsgVelNed MsgVelNed
   | SBPMsgUnknown MsgUnknown
  deriving ( Show, Read, Eq )

instance Binary SBPMsg where
  get = do
    preamble <- getWord8
    if preamble /= msgSBPPreamble then get else do
      t <- getWord16le
      void $ getWord16le
      l <- getWord8
      p <- getByteString $ fromIntegral l
      void $ getWord16le
      return $ decode' t p where
        decode' t p
          | t == msgAcqResult = SBPMsgAcqResult $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgAcqResultDepA = SBPMsgAcqResultDepA $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgBootloaderHandshakeReq = SBPMsgBootloaderHandshakeReq $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgBootloaderHandshakeResp = SBPMsgBootloaderHandshakeResp $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgBootloaderJumpToApp = SBPMsgBootloaderJumpToApp $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgNapDeviceDnaReq = SBPMsgNapDeviceDnaReq $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgNapDeviceDnaResp = SBPMsgNapDeviceDnaResp $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgBootloaderHandshakeDepA = SBPMsgBootloaderHandshakeDepA $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgExtEvent = SBPMsgExtEvent $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgFileioReadReq = SBPMsgFileioReadReq $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgFileioReadResp = SBPMsgFileioReadResp $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgFileioReadDirReq = SBPMsgFileioReadDirReq $ decode (fromStrict p)
          | t == msgFileioReadDirResp = SBPMsgFileioReadDirResp $ decode (fromStrict p)
```

@kaplun just noticed this issue and there are (unfortunatley) some points form the librarian.

- I perfectly agree with dehyphenation in searches and in the index.
- I fear your libs handle only _valid_ ISBNs. However, a lot of "formally invalid" ISBNs are around (e.g. wrong checknums etc.) This could quite a bit affect your re-hyphenation. Cf. [Library of Congress, 020](http://www.loc.gov/marc/bibliographic/bd020.html) and the section about "invalids".
- Re-hypenation can be quite an issue, as acutally IRL number groups overlap between small publishers and large publishers. So if you strip the hyphens for storage, IRL I think there is _no_ way to re-add them automagically and correctly in all cases. (Even if you treat formally valid ISBNs only). This was a real world problem for "potential dupe"-alerts in one of the univ. libraries I worked at. For me this would be a point like "if someone adds the hyphens keep them, but search and index without them". Plus: I'd not care to much about re-adding them if they are lost. Simply, as for the relevant cases the automatism fails anyway, and for the non-relevant case, well it's not relevant.
- Unlike a DOI that should be pointing to one article this is not the case for an ISBN. So I'd not really treat ISBNs as an ID as such. It's quite a bit "softer" than a real ID. You have a lot of cases where the same ISBN points to more than one book. Multi volume works are a very common case. Similarly subsequent editions, even if they incorporate major changes.

Due to the latter point I do not really know if it's worthwhile to put too much effort in this only half useful concept of _ISBN_. It works nicely for ordering a book that is currently available at the publisher. And only for them. All beyond (e.g. dupe checking and things) is probably asking way to much from this number. So I'd keep things simple and leave out all fancy libs.

Instead of setting/deleting ``account_info`` in the session in ``contrib/orcid.py``, it would better to do set ``account_info`` in ``authorized_signup_handler`` ([here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/pu/invenio/modules/oauthclient/handlers.py#L208)) and retrieve/delete it in ``signup_handler`` ([here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/pu/invenio/modules/oauthclient/handlers.py#L278) and [here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/pu/invenio/modules/oauthclient/handlers.py#L285)) .

Additionally, modify ``oauth_register()`` ([here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/pu/invenio/modules/oauthclient/utils.py#L63)) to accept ``form.data`` and ``account_info`` (passed in [here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/pu/invenio/modules/oauthclient/handlers.py#L279)).

This way every service (orcid, github, google, twitter), that uses the signup handler  doesn't need to set and retrieve the account info in the session.

This used to work before because versions markers were ignored by the requirements-builder < 0.2.2 and some version of setuptools, so specifying the version markers didn't do anything. functools32 even if not specified explicitly in dependencies, will still be fetched through ``invenio-records`` -> ``jsonschema`` dependency chain:
```
...
Collecting functools32; python_version == "2.7" (from jsonschema>=2.5.1->invenio-records>=1.0.0b1->invenio-oaiserver==1.0.0a11)
```

That's how Invenio-JSONSchemas is getting away with it, i.e.: by not specifying functools32 in the dependencies: https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-jsonschemas/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=functools32

I would suggest removing the dependency from setup.py completely just to fix it for now, and keep this issue pinned as generic "version markers do not work" problem.

This is in turn used by the `Term` class... (and is used in turn by `DesignInfo` class). `ModelDesc` has a list of terms (`lhs_termlist` and `rhs_termlist`), so adding this would be easy. 

I presume you're implying that I shouldn't be worrying about the `EvalEnvironment` variable and just return every dependent object--function and module alike? I was trying to return only "data"-ish things. Simply removing them from the output set manually set seems easy enough... 

 > The even simpler (from the API design / coupling perspective) would be to pass in a dict-like that loads the data lazily on demand, like:

This is really clever, thanks! I'll try it. However, I don't think it solves the `partial` issue below. 

> Is the partial part somehow tied to the var_names part? They look like separate changes to me, so should be in separate PRs?

Yes. `partial` looks at each `Term`'s `var_names` and decides whether the `Term` needs the variable or not. If yes, it pulls that `Term` using `subset` to create the design matrix only for that subset of columns using the variables specified. Otherwise, it returns columns full of zeros. The end result is a design matrix of the same width and column alignment  as the model's `DesignMatrix`, but only with as many rows as needed to evaluate the partial predictions and the rest of the columns as zeros.

Hi @guillett,

Sorry for the late reply.

We ran some performance test with @sandcha, and currently there is a significant performance drop if we activate the tracer by default. I don't remember the exact number, but it was around 20-30%.

The tracer doesn't fully duplicate the results, it keeps references towards the array contained by the holders. But it spends _a lot_ of time formatting strings to build the keys.

While there is certainly room for improvement, I don't think running the tracer all the time is the way to go. OpenFisca is already too slow in prod, so I'm reluctant to add more operations in the core of the reactor. 

What I'm more leaning towards though would be to have two modes
- `dev`: Tracer activated by defaults, nice error message guaranteed for everything, many checks on the legislation
- `prod`: Consider that the legislation is valid and not buggy. Whenever a check has a significant cost, skip it. Maybe in that case we don't even try to detect circular dependencies, and we just let the stack overflow happen.

As a regular OpenFisca user, how do you feel about this approach?

Thanks for reporting this need. Actually, there is already a way to indicate that. You can do something like:

```yaml
description: Age of majority (in years)
values:
  1945-01-01:
```

Here's a summary of the changes I had to make in my app to make it work with the Azure 2.0 endpoint.

* In calls to adal.AuthenticationContext, give the `api_version='2.0'` parameter.
* The `resource` parameter should be blank in adal calls
* Instead of adding a `resource` parameter, put resource-scoped permissions in the `scope` param to the `/authorize` request.
* The /authorize request also needs the 2.0 endpoint in the URL (or as a form param?)
* Scope may also need `openid`, `email`, `profile`, and `offline_access` depending on your app's needs.
* Be sure to set `response_type` correctly depending on if you need an access token (`code`), `id_token`, or both.
* You can't inspect the access tokens - they are opaque. 
* Be prepared to deal with some minor differences in the keys  in the identity token.
* When fetching the `/.well-known/openid-configuration` be sure to incorporate the correct tenant ID and endpoint version in the URL or you can get the wrong key.

These links may also help:

@jianchao123 @dengxixi 我也搞定了，谢谢二位。
ubuntu17.04系统下， 目前已经shell中使用curl命令，以及firefox中均能正常访问外网，

使用的正是 @jianchao123 提供的方法，简单总结如下：
1. shadowsocks使用socks5协议通信，firefox和curl等应用使用http协议或https协议通信。
2. 每次使用使用浏览器输入网址时，浏览器将数据组装成http格式的数据发送出去。
3. 由于shadowsocks只能接受socks5格式的数据，因此在收到浏览器发送的http格式的数据时，提示unsupported socks protocol version 67等等错误信息
4. firefox，curl等http应用需要polipo将http协议转换成socks5协议，才能使用shadowsocks代理上网

关于安装步骤简单说明如下：
1. 安装polipo，修改polipo配置并启动
2. 在shell中设置http_proxy变量，注意一定要使用export导出变量
3. 在shell中使用curl 访问ip.gs或ip.sb以验证两层代理工作正常
4. 如果要在firefox浏览器中使用，需要另外设置firefox的代理

I think there are two factors here: build-time caching and run-time caching.

`--disable-cache` will turn off build-time caching, which has a similar effect as the tox.ini setting above except without the tmp dir creation. setting `PEX_ROOT` (the dir `pex` explodes run-time caches to) to a new temp dir on each run should solve the run-time caching problem in the no-version-bump case.

so probably something like this should work:

```
PEX_ROOT=$(mktemp -d) pex --disable-cache --python python2.7 .
```

and then you could most likely rely on your OS to cleanup the tmp dirs after the fact.

It appears that Sonos uses `<item>` tags for things where the UPnP spec requires `<container>` tags, e.g. ` object.container.playlistContainer` is an `item` not a `container` !.  I have fixed a few tags, and improved the error messages to help track down this problem in future.  Please let me know if there are ongoing problems

I would definitely vote for consistent base 0, because after all this is a python library, and indices in python are base 0. Any other choice will inevitably irritate users at some point. I know the advantages of staying close to the protocol, but this isn't worth it.

For instance, if you have retrieved the queue as a list `queue` and want to start `queue[i]`, the right call should be `play_from_queue(i)` and not `play_from_queue(i+1)`. The same argument applies to the other functions: A track you have added via `add_to_queue(item, i)` should appear at `queue[i]`, not `queue[i-1]`.

The UIs look different (by design, probably) in the sign up form, edit modal, and profile editing. I think I advocated instead abstracting out clearly re-usable parts (like validation logic) rather than context-dependent stuff (like form layout).

There's a lot of discussion about "draining entopy" from `urandom`, but the conclusion from cryptographers (that I trust) seems to be: don't worry about that. (Of course, speed is a different thing).

From the paper @njsmith mentions, there *are* some proxies deployed on the internet that fail in that specific way (i.e. they don't understand the framing enough to know that the ping/pong payload bytes *aren't* a request). Not very many, but more than zero...so, an option to use `os.urandom` sounds like a decent compromise (if you're deploying a Python client in some weird or very-adversarial network topology).

I think "B" above (fixed all-0's mask) might be non-spec-compliant? (5.1 says "a client MUST mask all frames that it sends to the server").

So the current situation is that we're using `urandom`-seeded Mersenne Twister, right? Instead, we could just (always) use a `urandom`-seeded block-cipher as a (secure) PRNG. Do we unconditionally depend on PyNaCl? It has a `random_bytes` function with a salsa20 implementation (I'm not sure how you switch whether it's using the "systemrandom" or "salsa20" implementation from the python bindings though).

I'd recommend the `constructor` package, but I know that you're wrapping a custom installer around this, so I don't think that will work for you.
No problem! Achieving compatibility with `conda-forge` is something we are trying to do.

`activate` on windows should absolutely be adding `Library\bin` to `PATH`.

https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.2.16/conda/cli/activate.py#L73

How are you accessing a command line on windows? In what terminal?  How is the initialization of the terminal being configured?

If you're using the Anaconda Prompt that's a cmd.exe shell, then `activate root` should be called automatically for you.  If you're just using plain `cmd.exe`, then you might have to call it yourself.  I don't know what all the Anaconda installer adds to PATH by default, but I know there's a push to add even less.

Closing this issue with behavior as settled for conda 4.6, which makes the `conda` command available everywhere, even if the base environment is not and/or never has been active (most work done in conda 4.4), along with not stacking activated environments by default, but adding a `--stack` flag to `conda activate` as discussed in https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/7194.

I believe the ones that were in the environment previously are linked against Accelerate (system BLAS) on Mac, OpenBLAS on Linux, and MKL on Windows. In short, on Windows you should get the same result in either case.

Going forward Mac and Linux will have the same linkages, but now track the `nomkl` package. An `mkl` package has been added to those platforms so that you can use MKL linked versions. On Windows, there is no option to not use MKL so its version will appear as tracking `mkl`.

The `mkl-rt` package I don't believe is supported any more. It is getting pulled in by accident. Not sure why this happens, but have seen this also. This package is the reason you are getting old versions `numpy`, `scipy`, and `scikit-learn`. Removing it should be sufficient for getting back to the newer versions of those packages and keeping MKL if you wish.

Should add this doesn't seem to be unique to any of the channels. This just happens any time a channel beginning with `https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs` is used. Have tested the `defaults` channel URLs and `conda-forge` independently. All of them have this issue.

The fact that conda doesn't look at the dependencies of already installed packages when installing is a [known issue](https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/454). If that worked then conda would disallow installing both (actually, in this specific case, the libpng version restriction is not specified in the r-base package, so it would just use the older libpng and break R). 

The real issue here is that there's no way to install both libpng versions at once. I'm not sure if it's something that conda should try to solve or if it should always be solved from the packaging side.

I thought it was neater to fix in modelsim_interface.py. Seems there are weird constraints on the braces/brackets. Through some experimentation I've worked out:

Strings with a space require braces and quotes eg. `'{"x y"}'` or `'{"x, y"}'`.
String with a comma but no space must have quotes but no braces eg. `'"x,y"'`.
String with neither comma or space require no braces or quotes eg. `'xy'`.

Something like `-g/tb/output_path={"x,y"}` in common.do won't expand out properly for some reason.

Hi Travis. The logic should be something like:

1. Parse the record name
2. If the record name is relative, add on the current value of $ORIGIN (which can change from time to time), to get the FQDN
3. Remove the zone name (which stays constant throughout the import)
4. Remove trailing dot
5. Use the result as the record name (or if you have an empty name at this point, use '@' instead).

At step 3, the FQDN should *always* end with the zone name. If not, then there was an error earlier, either in the record name or in the value of $ORIGIN.

Summarizing some of the investigation in #628 

We attempted `--depth=1` for cloning and this hit two key snags:
1. If the revision in `.pre-commit-config.yaml` is not exactly master's sha, after cloning [checkout will fail](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/pull/628#issuecomment-331517451)
2. `autoupdate` works by inspecting history, there's no history when cloning with `--depth=1`

The test suite initially didn't highlight this due to `git clone /file/path` not supporting `--depth=1`.  This was adjusted in #629 and demonstrates failures with `--depth=1` in tests/commands/autoupdate_test.py (which seems to be the only tests which really exercised 1. above as well)

Cloning with `--depth=1` *is fast*, but we need to make sure it works correctly for the cases above.

In my analysis of the bugs in other products, the reason that this issue is pervasive in many projects is not that there's an unresolved problem in Samba. It's pervasive because it's hard to get right, because of various levels of caching at the Samba and filesystem layers and multiple opportunities to pull clock information from sometimes asynchronous sources. I believe that's why it manifests in VMs as well.

Eclipse uses native platform APIs to track file changes, like inotify I suppose. They decided to automatically background refresh non-dirty editor windows.

I don't know the codebase, but from snooping a bit, I can't find where the `FileContentsManager.save()` updates `model` with the last modified time after saving. It may be that this model returned from save() discarded and a new object is created with a subsequent call to get(), and maybe in the time between cals we see the issue.

Another possible approach that should be tolerant of clock skew and Samba bugs:
-calculate a hash for the file while saving
-if last modified timestamps indicate a change, and the time window is small, calculate the hash of the new file and compare to the hash of the last known saved file

Since notification of a file changed in the background doesn't need to be immediate, the hashes can be calculated in a background thread/task/coroutine. The hash of the last saved file doesn't even need to be saved, if a copy is left in memory until needed.

@joel-coffman Here's the description of the `DateTime` primitive from the KMIP spec (1.1):

`
Date-Time values are POSIX Time values encoded as Long Integers. POSIX Time, as described in IEEE Standard 1003.1 [IEEE1003-1], is the number of seconds since the Epoch (1970 Jan 1, 00:00:00 UTC), not counting leap seconds.
`

The examples in the spec and testing documents all use GMT time examples. As I said previously, we could move from using `time.ctime` in `DateTime.__str__` to using a combination of `time.gmtime` and `time.strftime`. That should fix the time zone problem. However, locales bundle in language information, so any sort of hard coded time string in a test case will break if a different locale is used because the language of the time string may be different. Hence why I wanted to dynamically generate the time string value in the first place.

Worked on a persistent metrics_to_dict function like suggested.
We should only use the letters `{'n', 'd','b', 'l', 'a', }` in the names of things.

You don't need to record a separate number of events per *data key*; you need to record it per *descriptor*. I would suggest adding a dictionary to the RunStop document mapping each descriptor uid to the number of events it encompasses (its max `seq_num`).

The information you need is tracked in the RunEngine's `_sequence_counters` attribute, which maps `(bundle_name, objs_read)` to an `itertools.count` object. Additionally, the `_descriptors` attribute maps these same keys -- `(bundle_name, objs_read)` -- to descriptor uid. By cross-referencing them, you can get the mapping you need.

Although this detail doesn't matter for your purposes, FYI, `bundle_name` is the same thing as `descriptor['name']` and `objs_read` is a `frozenset` of the ophyd objects (devices/signals) read for this descriptor. The `objs_read` is used internally by the RunEngine but, obviously, does not end up in the documents because devices can't be serialized. The *names* do go into the documents, as `descriptor['object_keys']`.

I've added a mention of the the `save()`/`get()`/etc methods like so:

    >>> server_config.save()  # Save to disk w/label 'default'. Read with get()

I want to make this example as brief as possible and give users the minimum necessary set of tools to get started. Simply saving and reading back the "default" configuration should be sufficient. Users can learn about the ability to work with multiple labels, the `get_labels` and `delete` methods, and other functionality as needed. The mention of "w/label 'default'" should hint that there's more to learn.

I can add a section mentioning SSL like so:

    ...     verify=False,  # Ignore SSL errors. DANGEROUS AND INADVISABLE.

That said, I must express a *strong* dislike for mentioning this feature in the top-level README. Ignoring SSL verification really is **dangerous and inadvisable**. When someone abuses the SSL system, the onus should be on them to make things work - we should not be teaching users that it is acceptable to undercut security technologies for convenience. Furthermore, it is likely that many people will simply copy and paste the provided examples and use the examples with little-to-no modification in their own code. Adding `verify=False` in to our examples means that other users will end up with insecure code - and they will rightly be able to blame us for providing bad advice.

@nicoddemus, @RonnyPfannschmidt @fschulze just had a convo with @vodik about this and I think we could make an alternative version of what a wrapper is for iterable hooks that's even more powerful.

Instead of having setup and teardown before and after **all** `hookimpl` calls are complete, instead define a wrapper as more of an *intercepting generator*. 

That is a hookwrapper is a generator which looks as follows:

```python
@hookimpl(wrapper=True):
def myproject_get_password(arg1, arg2):
    iter_results = yield
    for result in iter_results:
        # intercept and do something with each result
        yield result   # delivers the hookimpl call result to the hook caller (or next hookwrapper in line)
        # do something after the result has been delivered but before the next 
        # hook call has been invoked inside _itercall
```
Here `iter_results` is the generator returned from `_itercall()`.

We could also do some even more interesting stuff in py3.3+.  We **can** actually support the original wrapper semantics in a cleaner way with `yield from`:
```python
@hookimpl(wrapper=True):
def myproject_get_password(arg1, arg2):
    iter_results = yield
    # do the normal pre-call stuff
    try:
        results = yield from iter_results  # yields every hookcall upwards to caller; returns full list of yielded results
    except BaseException as err:
        if raise_on_err:
            raise err
    else:
        # do the normal post-call stuff
        results.clear()
        results.append('my_override_result')
        return results  # this could still be used for the normal (non-iterable) hook calls
```
Which has the extra nice nicety that we really wouldn't need the `pluggy.callers._Result` type any more.
We of course would offer these types of iterable hooks (and their corresponding wrappers) alongside the existing API and could encourage migration if we like the new version better?

Let me know what you guys think!

HPKP and DANE (and DNSSec) is **VERY** fragile. Need manual review after change and some time syncing not very simple to guess and automate (HPKP expiration delay for example).

> You don't need to pin the leaf certificate

Goal of HPKP and DANE is mainly to cancel the CA trust problem.
Pinning intermediate or root certificate is not the purpose.

> HPKP is based on the public key of the certificate used

Yep, and by default, Let’s Encrypt renew it ever 90d.
Possible to disable this behaviour, but as always now, we need « directly usable good configuration by default »

And for DANE, I agree, public key can be pinned, but also cert and currently not possible with Let’s Encrypt.

> It's easily scriptable to manage the DNS updates

No, not so simple with DNSSec (and DANE require it), and worse with KSK on a offline shadow master…

Okay, I rephrased the changelog entry.

@rdonnelly3 You don't need to call poll() to retain your group membership (which was the case in older versions of the Java consumer), a background thread is doing all the protocol level heartbeats, etc.
But you will need to call poll() to find out when the group rebalances (revoke + assign) so that your consumer is not processing messages for partitions it is no longer the assigned member for.
Pause/resume does not really change that so I don't believe it will be a magic bullet.

After a long-time processing of a message you find that the given partition has been revoked you should typically discard that message and not commit the offset, but start over with the next message returned from poll, coming from the new assignment.

For long time processing you should also disable automatic commits, since message offsets are stored for auto commit when the message is passed to the application which means that it is likely that the offset will be committed while you process the message.

Finally had some time to look into this. The issue here is that you need to also set the thermodynamic parameters for the internal oligo (the primer design process was using monovalent and divalent cation concentrations of 0 for the internal primer tm calculation, which explains the discrepancy you were seeing). Adding these args to your test script `p3_args` dict fixes things: 

```
'PRIMER_INTERNAL_DNA_CONC':           50,
'PRIMER_INTERNAL_SALT_DIVALENT':       1.5,
'PRIMER_INTERNAL_SALT_MONOVALENT':    50,
'PRIMER_INTERNAL_DNTP_CONC':           0.6
```

See also http://primer3.sourceforge.net/primer3_manual.htm#PRIMER_INTERNAL_SALT_MONOVALENT and similar entries in `primer3` manual. 

Full updated test script:

```
import primer3
if 1==1:
   p3_seq_args = {'SEQUENCE_TEMPLATE': ('AGCTGAACATACACGGCACAGAATACGCCGTCGATGTG'
      'CCnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnn'
      'nnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnGCCGTCTCACCGACGACGAACACCACTAGGCGTGGCTTACGC'
      'nnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnn'
      'nnnnnnATACTGGCAAGCTTACCCACC')}
   p3_args = {'PRIMER_PICK_INTERNAL_OLIGO': 1,
              'PRIMER_DNA_CONC':           50,
              'PRIMER_SALT_DIVALENT':       1.5,
              'PRIMER_SALT_MONOVALENT':    50,
              'PRIMER_DNTP_CONC':           0.6,
              'PRIMER_NUM_RETURN':          1,
              # Need to set separate thermo parameters for internal oligo
              'PRIMER_INTERNAL_DNA_CONC':           50,
              'PRIMER_INTERNAL_SALT_DIVALENT':       1.5,
              'PRIMER_INTERNAL_SALT_MONOVALENT':    50,
              'PRIMER_INTERNAL_DNTP_CONC':           0.6
              }
   p3_res = primer3.bindings.designPrimers(p3_seq_args, p3_args)
   fw_seq, fw_tm1 = p3_res['PRIMER_LEFT_0_SEQUENCE'], round(p3_res['PRIMER_LEFT_0_TM'],1)
   pr_seq, pr_tm1 = p3_res['PRIMER_INTERNAL_0_SEQUENCE'], round(p3_res['PRIMER_INTERNAL_0_TM'],1)
   rv_seq, rv_tm1 = p3_res['PRIMER_RIGHT_0_SEQUENCE'], round(p3_res['PRIMER_RIGHT_0_TM'],1)
   thal_type = 1
   mv_conc = p3_args['PRIMER_SALT_MONOVALENT']
   dv_conc = p3_args['PRIMER_SALT_DIVALENT']
   dntp_conc = p3_args['PRIMER_DNTP_CONC']
   dna_conc = p3_args['PRIMER_DNA_CONC']
   temp_c = 60
   ThermoAnalysis = primer3.thermoanalysis.ThermoAnalysis
   p3_therm = ThermoAnalysis(thal_type,mv_conc,dv_conc,dntp_conc,dna_conc,temp_c)
   fw_tm2 = round(p3_therm.calcTm(fw_seq),1)
   pr_tm2 = round(p3_therm.calcTm(pr_seq),1)
   rv_tm2 = round(p3_therm.calcTm(rv_seq),1)
   print("%25s  %17s  %17s"%('oligo', 'tm designPrimers', 'tm ThermoAnalysis'))
   print("%25s  %17s  %17s"%(fw_seq, fw_tm1, fw_tm2))
   print("%25s  %17s  %17s"%(pr_seq, pr_tm1, pr_tm2))
   print("%25s  %17s  %17s"%(rv_seq, rv_tm1, rv_tm2))
```

Actually, I figured out why the `i += 1` call was not important - in the outer `if`, we're already checking that `i == len_l`, which means that `l` consists of *all* the tokens in the string, so advancing the token index by 1 just moves us past the end of the string.

Presumably it was originally in the *outer* branch because the author wanted `i` to move past the end of the string in case something later moved the token index backwards? I could see keeping it in there for that purpose, but I'm not really sure it makes sense, so I'm just dropping it entirely.

I looked into supporting Py2/3 compatibility. It is far from trivial.

> [Django developer group discussing the deprecation](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/django-developers/NZskysjasx8/discussion)
> I ended up abstracting our usage of `inspect.getargspec()` into some utility methods in a django.utils.inspect module. Each method has a separate branch for Python 2 and Python 3. It's not ideal, but I think it's preferable to adding a short-lived dependency or vendored copy [...]

AFAIK `marshamallow` doesn't have any hard dependencies, so adding a back port like [`funcsigs`](https://github.com/testing-cabal/funcsigs), is probably not desirable.

I wanted to replicate the behavior of `getargspec()` with `signature()` to provide backwards compatibility, but I am afraid it is not possible due to the [documented behavior](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0362/#implementation) of `signature()` 

> [PEP 362 > Implementation](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0362/#implementation)
> If the object is a bound method, construct and return a new Signature object, with its first parameter (usually self or cls ) removed.

Although the `getmembers()` method allows getting the value of bound arguments like `self`, it does not expose the original name the parameter was given. This makes it impossible to replicate the behavior of `getargspec()` when a signature like `def __call__(me, arg1, arg2)` renames the `self` parameter.

Replicating the behavior of `signature()` with `getargspec()` should be possible, but the solution in the [implementation in the Django PR](https://github.com/django/django/pull/4846/files#diff-661c241427e347ab93c204317d4f68dcR37) seems over simplified. The `self` arg is not always present, which is a case they don't seem to consider.

Looking at the source of [funcsigs](https://github.com/testing-cabal/funcsigs/blob/master/funcsigs/__init__.py#L73) which replicates removing bound methods like `self`, reveals it is classifying the `kind` of the parameters.

```py
kind = params[0].kind
if kind in (_POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD, _POSITIONAL_ONLY):
    # Drop first parameter:
    # '(p1, p2[, ...])' -> '(p2[, ...])'
    params = params[1:]
```
[funcsigs/\_\_init\_\_.py#L73](https://github.com/testing-cabal/funcsigs/blob/master/funcsigs/__init__.py#L73)

In summary, providing compatibility between Python 2 and 3 requires back porting a non-trivial amount of the `signature` implementation for Python 2.

I have decided to start with creating minimal "gold" I-V dataset for perhaps a half dozen or so model parameter sets.

Bear with me as this is my first real foray into reliable computing. Using [pyinterval](http://pyinterval.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide.html), below is a notebook snippet where I generate I-V data pairs using the bishop88 technique for a SunPower module, then I calculate the residuals using enclosing intervals for all the computed pairs and model parameters. This produces list of residual intervals, all of which contain zero and which have a width that gives one more/less confidence as to the validity of the solution pair. So, I think asserting zero in the interval and the interval endpoints not too far from zero could be the acceptance criterion for creating the gold dataset. Using the dataset would then entail running a particular choice of i_from_v() or v_from_i() and verifying that the answer is not too far from the gold values (no interval analysis used here, keeping this fairly straightforward). I am still wondering about the spacing and breadth of the I-V pairs.

Feedback here is welcomed.

```python
v_oc_V = pvsystem.v_oc(rsh, rs, nnsvt, i_rs_A, i_ph_A)

i_A = i_ph_A - i_rs_A*np.expm1(v_d_V/nnsvt) - g_p_S*v_d_V
v_V = v_d_V - r_s_Ohm*i_A
for sol_pair in zip(v_V, i_A):
    print("{:+20.16f} V, {:+20.16f} A".format(sol_pair[0], sol_pair[1]))
```

I am sorry for the long time it took me to reply: I didn't have time to keep this conversation going lately, but it's a topic I really care about.

My main motivations for an API change are:

1. Theano comes with a powerful (yet badly documented) way to debug: `tag.test_values`. The current interface hides these debug tools from the user, making it difficult to fix e.g. shape errors. Numerical shape inference helps only partially most of the times, because many shape-mismatch errors depend on specific combination of input shapes and parameters and/or because variable input shape makes numerical shape inference useless.
2. When working with variable-sized images, especially with network with contractive and decontractive paths, one very often needs to check the symbolic shape of the intermediate feature maps to e.g. pad/crop them or set the parameters of the next layer. This cannot just be done in one global call to `get_output`, because the information is needed before the layer structure is complete. In my case for instance I ended up calling `get_output` multiple times on each intermediate feature map. From what I understood, this is not only painfully slow but it will also potentially generate multiple parallel Theano graphs if Theano fails to merge the graphs together.
3. Implementing the cache would lead to a more complicated code (note though that the final user would not be affected or even aware of it), but it would make the interface more natural to someone who comes from pure Theano and, most importantly, completely eliminate the risk that the user calls `get_output` in a way he should not. 
4. Trying to solve the potential misuse of `get_output` by documenting it better does not seem like the best solution to me: it should not be the user to adapt to the API, but rather the API to adapt to the user IMO.
