The easiest way I looked into having objects shared between jobs just required expanding or using the API in the `SharedDir` to store a list of `IGangaFile` objects and allowing it to call `IGangaFile.remove` when it's cleaning up after itself.
In my implementation I just told `GaudiExec` to place new objects in an attibute for the SharedDir as well as in the (hidden) attribute where it already knows about them in the application.
The idea was that after a few releases the hidden attribute could be correctly dropped and users would be none the wiser as well as users moving forward being able to clean-up after existing jobs. (Old versions of ganga still loading the jobs but not cleaning up when removing them).

I held off on touching SharedDir as at the time there was a lot of work being done to fix the index counting and a few other minor bugs. It may be formally better to have some new SharedObjects class for sharing files between jobs but I'm not sure how much work this would be and would be backwards incompatible.
We should still look into making a cleanup hook for Applications/backends and other objects to be called by the remove method in the job class.
(or generically a method for all GangaObject type classes which would be called when objects are removed from a database which would be the ideal solution imo.)

Yes, Keil historically has looked similar to IAR.  Luckily however, they
added support for GCC __attribute__ in the past few years in addition to
the legacy method, so that is what toolchain.h is currently leveraging.  If
someone is using a much older version, we can add detection for that and
handle accordingly (based on demand).

CodeWarrior unfortunately uses #pragma, which can't be triggered with
#define generally.  I'll move CodeWarrior to "unsupported" for now.

Visual Studio also uses #pragma, but luckily has __pragma equivalent which
should be usable from a #define.   I'll look into adding Visual Studio
support and testing it.

So the plan is to resolve the comments, hone the macros to the supported
subset, and update the pull request.

So semver is `Major.minor.patch`

 - Major: potentially backward incompatible
 - minor: new feature, backward compatible
 - patch: minor bug fixes.

Exceptions to this which will not concern you

 - Before `Major` is 1, that is in `0.m.p` version, any change in `m` can be backward incompatible
 - Before the very first minor is 1, that is `0.0.p`, any change in `p` can be backward incompatible (pure develop mode)

In python, version requirement operators are specified here:
    https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/

In node, it is here
    https://github.com/npm/node-semver

@wjakob continuing our conversation, let summarize the main technical points and respond to some issues here, and perhaps @jagerman can chime in with his thoughts. 

There are currently two options for interfacing with the GIL
1) CPython's `PyGILState` API 
2) pybind11's `gil_scoped_acquire` / `gil_scoped_release` API

These methods are incompatible and cannot be nested because they each maintain there own state and do not synchronize with each other. Unfortunately, as noted in [pybind11.h](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/9f41c8eade51f88331758e561a7c12cea0042463/include/pybind11/pybind11.h#L1732), the standard `PyGILState` API is limited and does not support some reasonable (but advanced) operations. Fortunately, the nesting that occurs will happen fairly infrequently because most python projects are small scripts and not large long-running systems.

In my opinion, because this incompatibility exists and most programs will not use the advanced features of the current interface, I believe that the pybind11 API should be changed to be *compatible by default* and expose a simple way to use the current interface that allows for advanced thread control. This way it would be the responsibility of the developer explicitly using the advanced features to ensure that nested calls were safe (or at least provide some warning or a note in the docs).

However, as @wjakob points out
> Simply switching to PyGILState is likely a show-stopper: it will break compatibility for software that uses any of the advanced features and thus will require a new major version (i.e. pybind11 3.x).

This is a valid point, breaking compatibility does require a major version change. I hope to convince you that its worthwhile to do so. My main points of my argument are:

* Most projects will not use the advanced threading features
* Most projects will not use nested calls
* However, I think it is more likely (especially as Python grows and becomes used in larger and larger systems) for nested calls to occur. Kitware's [KWIVER](https://github.com/Kitware/KWIVER) project is an example of one such system. Perhaps, I'm biased, but I don't view KWIVER as a project with special requirements. I view it as an example of a larger system that is emerging. 
* Pybind11 has become widely adopted by projects such as [caffe2](https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2) and [pytorch](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch) and is quickly becoming a standard in the community. 
* I believe standards should be *compatible by default* with the current overarching `CPython` standard.

> Some of personal projects actually critically depend on the improved GIL state API, so my excitement for such a change is very limited.

I very much understand this. Ideally we can come up with a good solution that makes upgrading to a new API simple. Of course this means that the new version must have an API to behave like the current version. (Alternatively we can petition for Python 4 to integrate the improved GIL API or we can wait for @larryhastings to finish his [gilectomy](https://github.com/larryhastings/gilectomy)). 

Hopefully, I've laid out a reasonable argument as to why changing the current behavior of pybind11 is desirable. As for discussing the specifics of those changes (maybe even with ones that don't require a major version bump) I'll make a post in #1276

@ohemorange

Case-by-case breakdown (w.r.t. the proposed changes):

1. User specifies `cert-name` only: behaviour doesn’t change; usage is printed and we quit.

2. User specifies `cert-path` only:
- The certificate gets revoked, as per usual.
- We ask if they are interested in deleting the relevant files.
- We ask them them to pick the correct `cert-name` (because I couldn’t figure out any other way to match a cert-name to a cert-path).
- Given the selected `cert-name`, we check that it matches `cert-path`. If so, deletion proceeds as usual, otherwise an error is raised.

3. User specifies both:

- Revocation proceeds as usual, then: given the specified `cert-name`, we check that it matches `cert-path`. If so, deletion proceeds as usual, otherwise an error is raised.

4. User specifies neither: behaviour doesn’t change; usage is printed and we quit.

In response to the question about what to do for other certs in that lineage: I don’t know what’s best, as I don’t have a good grasp on different revocation use cases and don’t want to make assumptions.

If we delete/revoke the entire lineage, then it’ll help with #4108. Another way we could deal with this is by not touching other certs in the lineage, and just telling the user what will happen/that they need to do something about them as suggested in #4108. 

-------

Another idea: maybe instead of trying to delete certs for users and trying to navigate potentially conflicting choices on their part, we could just suggest to them what to delete after revocation? We could just print the commands they’d need to run.

Version 2.2
-----------

Released June 2nd, 2018

-   Merged new and updated translations from the community.
-   Passing ``data_`` args to render a field converts all the
    underscores to hyphens when rendering the HTML attribute, not just
    the first one. ``data_foo_bar`` becomes ``data-foo-bar``. (`#248`_)
-   The :class:`~validators.UUID` validator uses the :class:`uuid.UUID`
    class instead of a regex. (`#251`_)
-   :class:`~fields.SelectField` copies the list of ``choices`` passed
    to it so modifying an instance's choices will not modify the global
    form definition. (`#286`_)
-   Fields call :meth:`~fields.Field.process_formdata` even if the raw
    data is empty. This makes :class:`~fields.StringField` default to an
    empty string instead of ``None``. (`#280`_)
-   Added a :class:`~fields.MultipleFileField` to handle a multi-file
    input. :class:`~fields.FileField` continues to handle only one
    value. The underlying :class:`~widgets.FileInput` widget gained a
    ``multiple`` argument. (`#281`_)
-   :class:`~fields.SelectField` choices can contain HTML (MarkupSafe
    ``Markup`` object or equivalent API) and will be rendered properly.
    (`#302`_)
-   :class:`~fields.TimeField` and
    :class:`html5.TimeField <fields.html5.TimeField>` were added.
    (`#254`_)
-   Improved :class:`~validators.Email`. Note that it is still
    unreasonable to validate all emails with a regex and you should
    prefer validating by actually sending an email. (`#294`_)
-   Widgets render the ``required`` attribute when using a validator
    that provides the ``'required'`` flag, such as
    :class:`~validators.DataRequired`. (`#361`_)
-   Fix a compatibility issue with SQLAlchemy 2.1 that caused
    :class:`~ext.sqlalchemy.fields.QuerySelectField` to fail with
    ``ValueError: too many values to unpack``. (`#391`_)

> We need to provide the user with the ability to create idempotent direct transfers. We can't stay with the current model. So it seems we have two choices then.

Just to emphasize my opinion, all APIs when possible should be idempotent, otherwise retries are not safe, the API for depositing is one that is also problematic.

> So far we were regarding the identifer only as something to be used by applications built on top of raiden and not as a part of our protocol. The original issue left its purpose of existence a bit vague.
> How would it work?

The protocols stay the same, neither the smart contract nor the network interactions need to change. This only need to affect the local node data.

> We could have a map of identifiers to direct transfers in flight and if another user request comes for a direct transfer that is in flight and in the map we simply ignore it.

I don't think we should just ignore it, for the REST API the server should hold the request until the transfer completes or the lock expires. This allows the client to restart.

Also the map should be local and only for the outgoing transfers, shared among direct and mediated transfers. As "local and for outgoing" I mean that transfers were the node is the mediator should not end up in the map, nor transfer that the node is the target. (not sure about the exchange tasks)

> Agreeing on the transferred amount and exposing that interface to the user to have the ability to do idempotent transfers is also possible. The problem with this approach is that it does not sound very intuitive to the user. How would it work?

Well, the client would need to track the latest transferred amount, and transferred amount with a lower value than the latest balance proof would just be ignored. I don't think it's a necessarily a good interface, just pointed it out as an alternative.

After a through review of the Keysight N52xx driver I came to the conclusion that a significant modification and improvement is necessary. The following issues need to be addressed in the old driver: 

1) There is no way to add a trace. In the example notebook it is assumed that some traces exist already. If this is not the case the driver fails when selecting the trace (e.g. `pna.trace("S21")`) will fail. This is absolutely unacceptable and this author is amazed something so broken got into the QCoDeS main branch.

2)  The current driver uses `ArrayParameter` to return trace data. This will be deprecated in due course. 

3) Many commands on the N52xx are channel specific, yet the current driver omits the channel numbers all together. This will make operations default to channel 1. There is no way to modify which channel we want to operate on. 

For this reason a new base class has been written. @spauka can you review this? Especially look at the new example notebook.

`path.py` is actually a wrapper for `os.path`, so both are OS independent.

But as you can see e.g. [here](https://github.com/pytorch/tnt/blob/master/torchnet/dataset/listdataset.py#L39) or (ironically in the same snippet I quoted earlier) [here](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/datasets/folder.py#L30) , everyone is really inclined to use the `/` character to describe a folder instead of `os.path.join` which will cause problems for windows users (who are waiting for pytorch ! :p ). 
So overloading the `/` operator into the `os.path.join`method is a really elegant way of dealing things for me.

Also `path.py` is already contained in anaconda so a lot of people already has it
